name: Bran the Blessed
description: Bran the Blessed, also known as Bendigeidfran, is a giant and king of Britain in Welsh mythology. He is the son of Llyr and Penarddun and is a central figure in the Second Branch of the Mabinogi, the tale of Branwen ferch Llyr. Bran is renowned for his immense size and strength, his wisdom, and his generosity. He is depicted as a benevolent ruler, willing to sacrifice himself for the well-being of his people. His most famous act is wading across the Irish Sea to rescue his sister Branwen, who was mistreated by her husband, the Irish king Matholwch. This act of brotherly love and his subsequent death after being wounded by a poisoned arrow are central to his tragic heroism. Bran's head, severed in battle, continued to speak and protect Britain for many years, buried beneath the White Hill in London. His story explores themes of family loyalty, revenge, and the enduring power of the human spirit in the face of adversity. He represents a powerful, yet tragic, figure whose influence shaped the landscape of Welsh mythology and continues to resonate today. His giant stature and commanding presence solidify his place as a formidable warrior and a compassionate leader. Bran's legacy lies in his selflessness and his willingness to bear the burden of protecting his kin and his kingdom, even unto death. He is a symbol of strength, sacrifice, and the enduring spirit of Wales.
subtitle: Giant King, Protector of Britain
achievements: Known for immense size and strength

keyMyths.title: Branwen ferch Llyr
keyMyths.description: Bran's sister Branwen is married to the Irish king Matholwch, but she is mistreated. Bran leads an army to Ireland to rescue her, resulting in a devastating war.

keyMyths.title: Bran's Head
keyMyths.description: After being mortally wounded, Bran asks his companions to cut off his head and bury it in London. His head continues to speak and protect Britain from invasion.

keyMyths.title: The Cauldron of Rebirth
keyMyths.description: The Irish possess a cauldron that can resurrect the dead, but they are reborn without speech. Bran's forces destroy the cauldron, ending its power.

sources.author: Gwyn Jones and Thomas Jones
sources.description: A translation of the medieval Welsh tales, including the Second Branch which features Bran the Blessed.
sources.text: A translation of the medieval Welsh tales, including the Second Branch which features Bran the Blessed.

sources.author: John Matthews
sources.work: Taliesin: Shamanism and the Bardic Mysteries in Britain and Ireland
sources.description: Explores the mythological and shamanic elements present in the tales of Bran and other Welsh figures.
